meta.title: 3-Phase Power Calculator | Electrical Engineering Guide | AluCalc
meta.description: Calculate active, reactive, and apparent power in 3-phase systems. Professional guide on Star (Wye) and Delta configurations.

seo.intro: 3-phase power is the backbone of industrial electrical distribution, providing a more efficient way to transmit electricity and power large motors compared to single-phase systems. This guide dives into the vector math and configurations of balanced 3-phase circuits.
seo.practical: Essential for sizing industrial generators, high-power HVAC systems, manufacturing plant floor distribution, and large electric motor specifications.
seo.example: Scenario: A 480V motor drawing 100A with a power factor of 0.85: P = 1.732 * 480 * 100 * 0.85 = 70,665 W (approx 70.7 kW).
seo.step_by_step:
1. Identify the Line-to-Line Voltage (V).
2. Measure the Line Current (I) passing through the conductors.
3. Determine the system Power Factor (cos φ).
4. Apply the √3 multiplier for 3-phase summation.

technicalSections.title: Wye (Star) vs. Delta Configurations
content: In a Wye (Y) configuration, all three phases connect at a common neutral point. This is ideal for mixed single and 3-phase loads as it provides two voltage levels.

In a Delta (Δ) configuration, phases are connected in a loop. Delta is commonly used in high-power industrial motors and transmission because it provides high starting torque and doesn't require a neutral wire.

technicalSections.title: Power Component Relationships
content: Modern electrical engineering distinguishes between three types of power: Active (Real) Power, Reactive Power (Magnetic/Inductive), and Apparent Power (Total Vector Sum).

technicalSections.title: Efficiency and Load Balancing
content: Phase imbalance occurs when current flows unevenly across L1, L2, and L3. This causes heating in the neutral conductor and significantly reduces motor lifespan due to opposing torque vectors.
